raw_text:
Margaret M.A. Shaw. Place in the Eclogues . Diss., Austin, University of
Texas, 1971.
1 1. J.B. Van Sickle, rev. T.G. Rosenmeyer, The Green Cabinet ; Theocritus
and the European Pastoral Lyric (Berkeley, Calif. 1969), AJP 93 (1972)
348-54.
Geymorat's text continues to underscore the authority of Sabbadini's original (Rome, 1930) throughout. Boyancé's lecture (2) offers a lucid 'reading'
of the text of Eclogue 1 deliberately avoiding trendy interpretations and
abstruse pronouncements in favor of obvious responses to the language and
sensibility of the poet. Clausen (3) sheds fresh light on the chronology of the
Eclogues in general in his particular study of the likely date for Eclogue I and
brings the Donatus-Suetonius chronicle into proper perspective. Flintoff (4)
examines the status of quo ( Eclogue 4, 8) and alters Mynor's punctuation in
O.C.T. to nascente, puero quo , implying an absolute construction or an ablative of circumstance. Schmidt (5, 9) provides much food for thought in his
sensitive study of the pastoral poems; his article contrasts the Theocritean
'retreat' from the city with the Vergilian 'return' to the urban, political
context. Bardon (7) examines political themes in bucolic poetry - civil war,
ruler-divinity assimilation, the golden age, and libertas. Rutledge (6) finds

title: 1 1. J.B. Van Sickle, rev. T.G. Rosenmeyer, The Green Cabinet ; Theocritus and the European Pastoral Lyric (Berkeley, Calif. 1969), AJP 93 (1972) 348-54. Geymorat's text continues to underscore the authority of Sabbadini's original (Rome, 1930) throughout. Boyancé's lecture (2) offers a lucid 'reading' of the text of Eclogue 1 deliberately avoiding trendy interpretations and abstruse pronouncements in favor of obvious responses to the language and sensibility of the poet. Clausen (3) sheds fresh light on the chronology of the Eclogues in general in his particular study of the likely date for Eclogue I and brings the Donatus-Suetonius chronicle into proper perspective. Flintoff (4) examines the status of quo ( Eclogue 4, 8) and alters Mynor's punctuation in O.C.T. to nascente, puero quo , implying an absolute construction or an ablative of circumstance. Schmidt (5, 9) provides much food for thought in his sensitive study of the pastoral poems; his article contrasts the Theocritean 'retreat' from the city with the Vergilian 'return' to the urban, political context. Bardon (7) examines political themes in bucolic poetry - civil war, ruler-divinity assimilation, the golden age, and libertas. Rutledge (6) finds 77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr

raw_text:
G. Zalateo, 'Da Omero a Virgilio', Atene e Roma 16 (1971) 120-6.
McDonald (13) treats Georgics 1, 395 to close analysis and translates the
verse as 'the sharp (projected) brilliance of starlight'. Martyn, editor of the
Hunt Festschrift (14), shows that, in two sections of the second Geòrgie,
Vergil developed a truly satirical attack on those perennial victims of the
satirist's lash, greed, luxury and vain ambition. McKay (15) offers a structural
analysis and commentary on Vergil's Laudes Italiae. Pieri (16), with reference
to Georgics 2, 458 ff., interprets the Vergilian allusions in Horace's Second
Epode as an evocation and a tribute. Brind 'Amour (17) substitutes pransique
for prensique in Georgics 3, 208 and supports his reading with complementary notices from Xenophon and Varro. Gervais (18) brings the plague
of Georgics 3 into a larger perspective. Detienne (19) approaches the OrionEurydice-Aristaeus 'triangle' from the standpoint of comparative religion and
sheds fresh light on the symbolic associations of honey and the Aristaeus
epyllion. Zalateo (23) treats Georgics 1, 382: densis . . . a/is as a derivative
but altered expression.

Apologists, detractors and detectives continue to struggle gamely with the
Appendix Vergiliana. Barrett's article (51) takes a reasonable and properly
critical view of the Suetonius-Donatus testimony. Clarke (52) argues that the
author of the Ciris (139 A.D.) was related to L. Valerius Messalla Thrasea
Priscus, consul : in 196 A.D. d'Eufemia (53) detects Vergilian borrowings in
